subject: This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI — Date of April 11, 2026
body:
This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI

Five papers worth your time at the intersection of real-world evidence, causal inference, and artificial intelligence.

TrialScout links published results to trial registrations using a large language model

von Schreeb et al. — medRxiv (April 2026)

Publication bias is not a theoretical concern — it is a systematic distortion of the evidence base, and TrialScout addresses it at industrial scale. von Schreeb and Nilsonne et al. deploy an LLM to match 9,600 ClinicalTrials.gov registrations against PubMed, finding results for 63.6% of trials at 92.5% sensitivity and 81.2% specificity against human coders. The 36% of trials with no findable publications is itself a research finding — and the automated pipeline makes reporting-compliance audits feasible for the first time at scale. Any systematic review team relying on registry screening as a publication-bias check should run TrialScout.

From Study Design to Executable Code: Automating Target Trial Emulation

Kim et al. — medRxiv (March 2026)

The bottleneck in target trial emulation is rarely understanding the method — it is the gap between writing a protocol and generating correct, runnable code across a real database. Kim et al. test eight LLMs on their ability to translate TTE study descriptions into executable R scripts, validating across 15 OHDSI studies with external validation on five more. Code executability improved substantially after LLM self-auditing, but the performance gap between models and settings is the crucial finding: not all LLMs are created equal for this task. With an interactive prototype already publicly available, this paper marks the beginning of AI-augmented TTE implementation workflows.

Estimating the impact of Shigella vaccines on growth outcomes and implications for clinical trial design

Codi et al. — medRxiv (April 2026)

David Benkeser and colleagues demonstrate the underappreciated third use case for TMLE: not just estimating causal effects from observational data, but using those estimates to power the next randomized trial. Applied to Shigella vaccine effects on linear growth faltering, the analysis generates the expected-effect-size distributions that Phase 3 trial designers need to choose sample sizes. This is the template for using causal estimation from existing data to avoid underpowered trials in neglected diseases, where there is no luxury of preliminary RCT data. The methodology transfers directly to any setting where regulatory and public health decision-makers need evidence-based power calculations before committing to a large trial.

Extracting Social Determinants of Health from Electronic Health Records: Development and Comparison of Rule-Based and Large Language Model Methods

Wang et al. — medRxiv (April 2026)

Social determinants of health are among the most consequential unmeasured confounders in pharmacoepidemiology, and they exist almost entirely in unstructured clinical notes. Wang, Smoller, and colleagues at Harvard/MGH run the head-to-head comparison practitioners have been waiting for: rule-based extraction versus LLM-based approaches on real EHR text. LLMs outperform rule-based methods on nuanced, contextually dependent SDoH categories (housing instability, food insecurity), while rule-based methods remain competitive for well-defined categorical variables. As regulatory agencies increasingly expect SDoH adjustment in RWE submissions, this comparison defines the minimum acceptable approach for each SDoH category.

Natural language processing to intercept rheumatoid and psoriatic arthritis from clinical notes in the Emergency Department

Tonutti et al. — Therapeutic Advances in Musculoskeletal Disease (April 2026)

This paper should be required reading before any team launches an NLP-based case ascertainment study in routine clinical data. Tonutti et al. build NLP models to detect RA and PsA signals from emergency department notes using propensity-score-matched controls — and watch performance collapse from AUC 70% in training to 62% in independent test sets. The drop is not a bug in the methodology; it is the expected consequence of deploying models on notes with different distributions than training data — precisely what happens in every real-world deployment scenario. The practical implication: NLP-based phenotyping algorithms need prospective external validation before they anchor any pharmacoepidemiology denominator.
